text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

One thing that filled me with joy was the genuine camaraderie between different competitors at a time when society feels divided

TheParis Olympicsbrought us all a lot of joy. Even if we are not usually sports fans, one thing shone throughout that event – and that was a sense of community. The Paris 2024 Olympics brought together a global community – and at a time when the world was feeling divided.

One thing that filled me with joy while watching was the genuine camaraderie between different competitors.

In a society that often pushes the idea that in order to be we must put others down, it was refreshing to see the opposite. Despite being competitors, theirempathy for others shone through– as did their ability to rise above success and instead be kind, supportive and respectful.

This translates well into why we need to stop and reflect about what success is, and change the idea of how we want to live, work and be, not just for others but also for our own health and wellbeing.

The concept of competition and survival of the fittest in evolutionary biology is one that we study when it comes to other ecosystems.

We talk about animals and plants needing to compete for light or food or resources. This is either between different species – interspecific – or betweenindividuals of the same species– intraspecific.

Intraspecific competition is often described as being more intense and having significant impact because the individuals and their role or niches overlap more.

Humans have this competitive evolutionary drive and very occasionally, in moments of real crisis, this drive and reaction is absolutely necessary. However, in most scenarios – like at work, in traffic jams, friendshipgroups or family dynamics– this competition is not necessary.

In fact, it can actually be to your detriment. An evolutionary drive that was designed to bring you success, happiness and sustainability, actually causes the opposite. It can cause division, unhappiness, lack of support and stress.

It can actually impact your mental and physical health. Take the example of a working environment where individuals in a team are pitted against each other. Up to a point, this can increase and improve output, but there is a tipping point where that then breeds toxicity, relationship dysfunction and even bullying or other behaviours that are not good for mental wellbeing.

We can celebrate our competitors and still becompetitive with them in a healthy way. To do this we need to see beyond the illusions that society draws.

Unlike animal and plant systems, in most circumstances where you are either encouraged to be competitive in a toxic way or you choose to be, there are actually enough resources for everyone. That might be space to talk in a meeting or time for your to progress in your role.

In your friendship groups,there is likely enough time, space and energy for everyone’s life updates and no need to take over the conversation or to put someone else down. In a family unit, instead of parents “favouring” one child and encouraging sibling rivalries, there is enough love and praise to go around. Most resources in these scenarios – like love, time, space and recognition- are plentiful.

We think that the only thing that will benefit us is if we succeed in the way we think we should. But praising others when they do well improves our sense of teamwork and the quality of our relationships.

We can alsolearn from others in their success– what they did well, how we can improve and take advice from them. We are also more likely to get praise and validation from others if we have treated them well, and support if we encounter a difficult time later, thus future-proofing our wellbeing.

Giving praise to others, validating them, being kind and celebrating them actually has benefits for our own wellbeing. Research has shown that giving compliments increases our own happiness, creates and cultivates gratitude.

It also has been shown to strengthen our personal relationships which are a mainstay of our physicaland emotional health. When we experience more positive emotions than negative ones – like jealousy, resentment or anger – we also protect our health and wellbeing, as the predominant type of emotion we feel is a strong predictor of wellbeing.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: The Paris Olympics taught us that competition doesn't need to be cruel
author: Doctor Radha
description: One thing that filled me with joy was the genuine camaraderie between different competitors at a time when society feels divided
